15.1 The Basic Processes of Photosynthesis

The Carbon Cycle (1 of 2)
Life cannot depend on oxidative metabolic processes alone; reverse (reductive) processes must occur
Photosynthesis includes processes in which light provides the large amount of free energy required to synthesize carbohydrates from
and water
Photosynthesis fixes
and is the major source of
atmospheric

The Carbon Cycle (2 of 2)
Figure 15.1 The carbon cycle in nature.

Photosynthesis in Metabolism (1 of 2)
Photosynthesis is the ultimate source of energy for almost all life
The major biosynthetic pathways leading from
and water topolysaccharides are shown in green
Figure 15.2 The role of photosynthesis in metabolism.

Photosynthesis in Metabolism (2 of 2)
Molecular oxygen is derived from water, and is released as a by-product of photosynthesis
Figure 15.2 The role of photosynthesis in metabolism.

Examples of Photosynthetic Reactions (1 of 3)
The general photosynthetic reaction can be written as:
is a general reductant and A is the oxidized product)

Examples of Photosynthetic Reactions (2 of 3)
All photosynthetic processes require a reductant; the table below shows several different examples

Examples of Photosynthetic Reactions (3 of 3)
Where water is the reducing agent, we can write the photosynthetic reaction as shown here to illustrate that
one of the oxygen atoms in
ends up in carbohydrate,

The Basic Process of Photosynthesis (1 of 3)
Photosynthesis can be divided into two subprocesses – light reactions and dark reactions
Figure 15.3 The two subprocesses of photosynthesis.

The Basic Process of Photosynthesis (2 of 3)
The light reactions use the energy from sunlight to oxidize
to
producing
A T P and N A D P H in the process
Figure 15.3 The two subprocesses of photosynthesis.

The Basic Process of Photosynthesis (3 of 3)
The dark reactions utilize
A T P and N A D P H to fix
(the reductive synthesis of carbohydrate)
These reactions are so named because they do not directly require sunlight (they do not occur only in the dark!)
Figure 15.3 The two subprocesses of photosynthesis.
15.2 The Chloroplast

Chloroplasts as the Organelles of Photosynthesis in Plants and Algae
Transmission electron micrograph (T E M) of a plant cell showing many chloroplasts within the cell
Figure 15.4 Chloroplasts, the photosynthetic organelles of higher plants and algae.

Chloroplast Structure (1 of 4)
Chloroplasts, like mitochondria, contain their own D N  A
They have three membranes – inner, outer, and thylakoid
Figure 15.4 Chloroplasts, the photosynthetic organelles of higher plants and algae.
Enlarged view of a chloroplast
Schematic rendering of a chloroplast

Chloroplast Structure (2 of 4)
Thylakoids are flat, sac-like structures that are often stacked like coins to form units called grana
Grana are connected via stroma lamellae
Figure 15.4 Chloroplasts, the photosynthetic organelles of higher plants and algae.
Enlarged view of a chloroplast
Schematic rendering of a chloroplast

Chloroplast Structure (3 of 4)
The stroma is analogous to the mitochondrial matrix and surrounds the grana
Carbohydrates produced by the dark reactions are typically stored as starch in the stroma
Figure 15.4 Chloroplasts, the photosynthetic organelles of higher plants and algae.
Enlarged view of a chloroplast
Schematic rendering of a chloroplast

Chloroplast Structure (4 of 4)
Absorption of light and the light reactions occur in the chloroplast membranes; dark reactions occur in the stroma
Figure 15.4 Chloroplasts, the photosynthetic organelles of higher plants and algae.
Enlarged view of a chloroplast
Schematic rendering of a chloroplast
15.3 The Light Reactions

Energy of Light (1 of 3)
Light can be characterized by its wavelength (λ) or its frequency (ν); wavelength and frequency are related by the following equation:
where c is the speed of light
A light beam can be thought of as a stream of particles called photons

Energy of Light (2 of 3)
The value of energy per photon (E) is called a quantum and is related to the frequency of light by the following equation:
where h is Planck’s constant,

Energy of Light (3 of 3)
The energy in a single photon of red light with a frequency of
would be:
One mole of photons is called an einstein; the energy in 1 einstein of these photons is:
= 189
kJ/mol of photons or 189 kJ/einstein

Energy and Wavelength
Infrared photons do little except stimulate molecular vibrations, which are perceived as heat
Photons in the far-ultraviolet range possess sufficient energy to break covalent bonds
Photosynthetic pigments capture light in the near-IR and visible range
Figure 15.5 The energy of photons.

Selected Photosynthetic Pigments and Their Absorption Spectra (1 of 2)
Figure 15.6 Some photosynthetic pigments.
Chlorophyll a and b are the most abundant plant and algal pigments; β-carotene and lutein are accessory pigments
Chlorophyll and some accessory pigments are located in the thylakoid membranes

Selected Photosynthetic Pigments and Their Absorption Spectra (2 of 2)
Figure 15.7 Absorption spectra and light energy.

Energy Transfer in Photosystems (1 of 2)
Light-harvesting pigments and their associated proteins are organized into membrane-embedded multi subunit complexes called photosystems
Plants use two distinct photosystems (P S  Ⅰ and Ⅱ  ); P S I and P S  Ⅱ contain antenna pigment molecules (such as chlorophylls) bound to light-harvesting complexes (L H C s) and a pair of special chlorophyll molecules which act as a reaction center

Energy Transfer in Photosystems (2 of 2)
When absorbing molecules are tightly packed, the energy of the excited molecule may be transferred to an adjacent molecule (resonance transfer) or the excited electron itself may be transferred to a nearby molecule with a slightly lower excited state (electron transfer)
Figure 15.8 Two modes of energy transfer following photoexcitation.

Structure of the L H C Ⅱ  Trimer (1 of 2)
Figure 15.9 Three-dimensional structure of the trimeric light-harvesting complex Ⅱ of plants.

Structure of the L H C Ⅱ Trimer (2 of 2)
Figure 15.9 Three-dimensional structure of the trimeric light-harvesting complex Ⅱ of plants.

Energy Transfer in an L H C
Photons of light are absorbed by antennae pigment molecules and rapidly transferred to a reaction center
The reaction center excited state energy level is slightly lower, trapping the quanta of energy
Excitation of the reaction center starts a series of electron transfer reactions
Figure 15.10 Resonance transfer of energy in a light-harvesting complex.

Path of Electrons Through Two Linked Photosystems (1 of 2)
Photosystems (P S s) were named in order of their discovery; P S Ⅰ absorbs light up to 700 nm and P S Ⅱ  absorbs light up to 680 nm
In each P S, the primary step is transfer of a light-excited electron to a reaction center

Path of Electrons Through Two Linked Photosystems (2 of 2)
Figure 15.11 The two-photosystem light reactions.

Energetics of the P S Ⅱ/Ⅰ-Dependent  (Noncyclic) Light Reactions
Figure 15.11 The two-photosystem light reactions.

The Cytochrome b 6  f Complex
The cytochrome
complex is analogous to the mitochondrial
complex Ⅲ It catalyzes the transfer of electrons from plastoquinol to plastocyanin.
Figure 15.12 Structure of the
cytochrome
complex.
Cytochrome
Complex Mitochondrial complex Ⅲ

Structure of the Photosystem Ⅱ Core
Figure 15.13 Structure of photosystem Ⅱ core.

Model for the Oxygen-Evolving Complex (O E C) (1 of 2)
The O E C contains a cube-shaped cluster with four Mn ions and one calcium ion bridged by oxygen atoms
Figure 15.13 Structure of photosystem Ⅱ core.

Model for the Oxygen-Evolving Complex (O E C) (2 of 2)
This metal cluster can exist in a series of oxidation states
Light-driven cycling through the oxidation states provides the energy for the dismantling of
Figure 15.14 A model for the catalytic function of the oxygen-evolving complex (O E C) cluster in P S Ⅱ  .

Photosystem Ⅰ (P S Ⅰ)
P S Ⅰ receives electrons from photosystem II and transfers
them to
to form N  A D P H
P S Ⅰ is a multiprotein complex that spans the thylakoid membrane
P S Ⅰ consists of the core complex and L H C I
In the last stage of transfer, reduced soluble ferridoxin
transfers electrons to
N  A  D P H is produced in the stroma

Structure of the Plant Photosystem Ⅰ
Figure 15.15 The structure of plant photosystem Ⅰ.

Summary of Light Reactions (2 of 3)
About 12 moles protons are pumped from the stroma into the thylakoid lumen per mole of
released
This energy in this proton gradient is used to generate AT P synthesis (similar as seen in the mitochondrial respiratory chain)
About 4 moles of A T P are generated per mole of
released

Summary of Light Reactions (3 of 3)
Figure 15.16 Summary view of the light reactions as they occur in the thylakoid.

An Alternative Light Reaction – Cyclic Electron Flow (1 of 2)
The electrons transferred by P S Ⅰ  are replenished by P S Ⅱ
in noncyclic electron flow; however, when
levels
are low, the electrons are passed back to P700 via
cytochrome
and plastocyanin
Approximately 1 A T P is produced for every two electrons
transferred, but no
is released and no N A D P H is
produced

An Alternative Light Reaction – Cyclic Electron Flow (2 of 2)
Figure 15.17 Cyclic electron flow.

The Single Photosystem in Purple Bacteria
Figure 15.18 Postulated mechanism for purple bacterial photosynthesis.
Purple bacteria contain a single photosystem that resembles P S Ⅱ . In this photosystem, water is not split and electron flow is cyclic.
15.4 The Carbon Reactions: The Calvin Cycle

Schematic View of the Dark Reactions (Calvin Cycle) (1 of 2)
The Calvin cycle uses the A T P and N A D P H generated in the light reactions to fix atmospheric
into
carbohydrate
The cycle can be divided into two stages:
Ⅰ
fixation and sugar production
II
– Acceptor regeneration

Schematic View of the Dark Reactions (Calvin Cycle) (2 of 2)
Figure 15.19 Schematic view of the Calvin cycle.

Calvin Cycle Stage I (1 of 4)
Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate (R u B P) accepts
Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase (Rubisco) catalyzes this complex reaction, producing two
3-phosphoglycerate molecules

Calvin Cycle Stage I (2 of 4)
In the following reactions of this stage, the
3-phosphoglycerate molecules are phosphorylated by phosphoglycerate kinase to produce 1,
3-bisphosphoglyercate, which are then reduced to
glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate (G A P) by glyceraldehyde 3 phosphate dehydrogenase

Calvin Cycle Stage I (3 of 4)
The synthetic pathway follows a portion of the gluconeogenic pathway
G A P is isomerized to dihydroxyacetone phosphate ( D H A P) by triose phosphate isomerase
G A P and D H A P are combined by fructose bisphosphate aldolase to form fructose 1,6-bisphosphate (F B P)
F B P is dephosphorylated by fructose 1,6-bisphosphatase to yield fructose-6-phosphate (F 6 P)

Calvin Cycle Stage I (4 of 4)
F 6 P is first isomerized to glucose-6-phosphate (G 6 P) and then to glucose-1-phosphate (G 1 P)
G1P is the precursor to the storage polysaccharide amylose:

Calvin Cycle Stage II (1 of 2)
The six R u B P molecules (30 carbon atoms) needed to continue the cycle are obtained from two hexoses (two F 6 P molecules; 12 carbon atoms) and six trioses (two D H A P molecules and four G A P molecules; 18 carbon atoms)

Calvin Cycle Stage II (2 of 2)
The final step is the phosphorylation of ribulose 5-phosphate by ribulose-5-phosphate kinase to form R u B P
The equation for the overall dark reaction is then:

Stoichiometry in the Calvin Cycle (1 of 2)
On a “per glucose” basis, six
molecules will
enter the Calvin cycle and
form 12 G A P molecules
Figure 15.20 Stoichiometry of the Calvin cycle.

Stoichiometry in the Calvin Cycle (2 of 2)
Of the 12 G  A  P molecules, only two will be used in the synthesis of hexose; the remaining 10 G  A  P molecules will be used to regenerate six R u B P molecules needed for the cycle to continue
Figure 15.20 Stoichiometry of the Calvin cycle.
15.5 A Summary of the Light and Carbon Reactions in Two-System Photosynthesis

Summary of Light and Dark Reactions (1 of 2)
The equation for the light reactions may be approximated* as:
*48 photons must be absorbed for the formation of N A D P H. We assume here that these 48 photons are sufficient to pump enough protons to synthesize the 18 A T P required

Summary of Light and Dark Reactions (2 of 2)
The equation for the dark reactions is:
Adding these two equations yields the net reaction:

Regulation of Photosynthesis (1 of 3)
Ribulose bisphosphate carboxylase (Rubisco) is stimulated by high pH and by both
and
As protons are pumped out of the stroma into the thylakoid lumen, pH of the stroma increases
ions enter the stroma to compensate for the
loss of the positive charges
Figure 15.21 Regulation of Rubisco by light.

Regulation of Photosynthesis (2 of 3)
Rubisco is activated by Rubisco activase, which is like other enzymes required for the light or dark reactions of photosyntesis activated by a ferredoxin/thioredoxin-dependent disulfide reduction pathway
Rubisco is sensitive to transition-state inhibitors such as 2-carboxy-D-arabinitol-1-phosphate or C A 1 P
Figure 15.21 Regulation of Rubisco by light.

Regulation of Photosynthesis (3 of 3)
Rubisco activase, fructose 1,6-bisphosphatase, glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate dehydrogenase, and ribulose 5-phosphate kinase are activated by the reduction of disulfides to sulfhydryls by a ferredoxin/thioredoxin-dependent pathway
Figure 15.22 Light-dependent activation of carbon-reaction enzymes.
15.6 Photorespiration and the C 4 Cycle

Low C O 2 and High O 2 (1 of 2)
Under conditions of low
and high
plants exhibit
photorespiration, in which
is consumed and
released
is consumed under these conditions because Rubisco
behaves as an oxygenase, incorporating
into R u B P
rather than
to form 2-phosphoglycolate
In photorespiration, R u B P is lost,
is consumed and
is released, A T P is expended and only 75% of the carbon is returned to the chloroplast

Low C O 2 and High O 2 (2 of 2)
Figure 15.23 Photorespiration.

Conserving C O 2 in C 4 Plants (1 of 2)
Some plants
have evolved an additional
photosynthetic pathway to help conserve the
lost in photorespiration
In the
cycle,
is coupled to phosphoenolpyruvate
forming oxaloacetate
Oxaloacetate is reduced to malate, which is decarboxylated in the bundle sheath cells and transfers
to the Calvin cycle

Chapter 15 Summary (1 of 3)
Photosynthesis captures electromagnetic radiation and converts it into chemical energy of reduced organic compounds
It accounts for all fixation of atmospheric
into
biological molecules and the production of nearly all
atmospheric
Photosynthetic processes can be divided into light reactions and dark reactions
light reactions use electromagnetic radiation (from sunlight) to extract electrons from
producing
reducing potential, and a proton gradient that drives
A T P synthesis

Chapter 15 Summary (2 of 3)
dark or carbon reactions reduce
into
carbohydrates
In higher plants and algae, both types of reactions take place in the chloroplasts
Energy from absorption of photons by antenna pigments is first transferred to reaction centers and then to photosystems (P S) I or II
P S Ⅱ oxidizes
while P S Ⅰ reduces
Both drive the transport of protons across the thylakoid membrane, providing a p H gradient to produce A T P via the chloroplast A T P synthase

Chapter 15 Summary (3 of 3)
The dark or carbon reactions are accomplished by the Calvin cycle, which can be divided into two stages
In the first stage,
is added to ribulose
1,5-bisphosphate (R u B P), which is then cleaved and reduced to triose phosphates that are then combined
to form hexose phosphates
In the second stage, most of triose phosphates and hexose phosphates are used to regenerate R u B P for the first stage, some is converted into starch (or other carbohydrates)
